_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 10-8 Hazardous/Contaminated Patient (MDHHS Reviewed 2022-12-27). Base Michigan template.
protocol_reference: Michigan Special Operations Protocol Section 10-8: Hazardous / Contaminated Patient — MDHHS Reviewed 2022
purpose: Establish EMS procedures for identifying, managing, and safely treating patients who are potentially contaminated with hazardous materials, including scene safety, decontamination coordination, and transport decisions.

sections.title: Contamination Identification
content: A patient is considered POTENTIALLY CONTAMINATED when:
— Found at or near a known or suspected hazmat scene
— Presents with symptoms consistent with chemical, radiological, or biological exposure
— Has unexplained burns, skin changes, or odors suggestive of chemical exposure
— Responds from an area where hazmat release has been identified or is suspected
— Emergency dispatch or law enforcement advises of potential contamination
ASSUME CONTAMINATION if uncertain — do NOT approach without appropriate PPE

sections.title: Initial EMS Actions — Contaminated Patient
content: 1. Do NOT approach contaminated patient without appropriate PPE
2. Notify dispatch, fire/HazMat, and online medical control immediately
3. Establish safe upwind/uphill staging position
4. Identify hazard type if possible — CHEMTREC (1-800-424-9300), ERG (Emergency Response Guidebook), placard information
5. Establish hot/warm/cold zone perimeter with fire/HazMat
6. Do NOT transport contaminated patient — decontamination must occur FIRST
7. Contact online medical control for destination hospital guidance

sections.title: Decontamination — EMS Role
content: DECONTAMINATION IS THE RESPONSIBILITY OF FIRE/HAZMAT — EMS does NOT perform technical decontamination
EMS role in decontamination:
— Advise on medical priorities for decon order (most critical patients first)
— Provide medical monitoring during decon if PPE-equipped
— Receive patients at decon exit (warm/cold zone boundary) for medical treatment
— Gross decontamination (clothing removal + water irrigation) is the minimum before EMS treatment
Clothing removal removes approximately 80% of surface contamination — all clothing removed, placed in labeled biohazard bags
Do NOT initiate medical treatment (IV, intubation, medication) on undecontaminated patient unless immediately life-threatening AND provider has appropriate PPE

sections.title: Invasive Procedures — Contaminated Patients
content: AVOID invasive procedures on undecontaminated patients:
— IV/IO access, intubation, needle decompression, wound packing — deferred until decontamination complete
— Exception: immediately life-threatening condition (cardiac arrest, tension pneumothorax, uncontrolled hemorrhage) where decontamination cannot occur first
— Exception: provider has appropriate Level B or C PPE for the identified agent
If performing invasive procedure on undecontaminated patient: use maximum available PPE; limit personnel exposure; document exception and rationale
Sharps handling: extreme care with contaminated patients — sharps injury creates secondary contamination risk

sections.title: Transport — Contaminated Patient
content: DO NOT TRANSPORT UNDECONTAMINATED PATIENTS in ambulance
Transport only after gross decontamination (clothing removal + water rinse) at minimum
Contact online medical control for destination hospital BEFORE transport:
— Not all hospitals can receive contaminated or partially decontaminated patients
— Hospital must be notified of agent type, decontamination status, and ETA
— Some agents (radiological, CBRNE) require hospital-level decontamination facilities
Ambulance contamination: if contaminated patient transported, ambulance is out of service until decontaminated per Protocol 10-15 or agency HazMat decon procedures
Notify dispatch of ambulance contamination status

sections.title: Specific Hazmat Types — Additional Guidance
content: CHEMICAL (nerve agent, vesicant, choking, cyanide): refer to Protocols 10-2, 10-3, 10-5 — decontaminate before treatment

RADIOLOGICAL: limit time/distance/shielding; dosimeter monitoring; decontaminate with water; hospital must have radiological decon capability; transport not delayed for stable patients after gross decon
BIOLOGICAL: standard precautions + suspected pathogen PPE; potential for delayed symptom onset; notify public health; refer to Protocol 10-9 if pandemic suspected
INDUSTRIAL/UNKNOWN CHEMICAL: identify material before approach; ERG/CHEMTREC guidance; treat symptoms after decon
CLANDESTINE LAB: law enforcement scene — extreme PPE required; multiple hazards (chemical, explosive); do NOT enter until cleared by law enforcement and HazMat

sections.title: EMS Provider Exposure
content: If EMS provider has potential exposure: exit scene immediately; notify IC and online MC
Remove and bag potentially contaminated clothing
Water irrigation for skin/eye exposure
Seek medical evaluation
Nerve agent self-exposure: administer 1 NA kit (Duodote) if symptomatic — notify IC
Document exposure incident; file agency exposure report
Post-exposure medical monitoring per agency and MCA protocols

key_drugs: Agent-specific antidotes per Protocols 10-3 (nerve agent) and 10-5 (cyanide) — after decontamination or with PPE
deterioration_flags: Life-threatening injury in undecontaminated patient — consider immediate intervention with PPE; document exception
EMS provider develops symptoms after potential exposure — exit scene; notify IC; initiate decontamination
Unknown agent with multiple casualties — do NOT approach; stage; await HazMat identification
scope_notes: EMS must NOT transport undecontaminated patients — contaminating the ambulance removes it from service and creates secondary exposure risk. Decontamination is fire/HazMat responsibility. Online medical control must be contacted for destination guidance — not all receiving hospitals can accept contaminated patients. Invasive procedures are deferred until decontamination is complete except in immediately life-threatening scenarios with appropriate PPE.
